name: Abdullah ibn Ja'far
epithet: The Son of the Man of Two Wings
title: Abdullah ibn Ja'far (RA): The Son of the Man of Two Wings
meta_description: The story of Abdullah ibn Ja'far (RA), born in Abyssinia to refugees, son of the martyr Ja'far. A life that touched the great events of early Islam, carried with patience to the end.
keywords: Abdullah ibn Jafar, story of Abdullah ibn Jafar, son of Jafar ibn Abi Talib, man of two wings, sahaba stories, The Firsts, companions of the Prophet
lede: Some lives stay in one place. This one moved through almost every great event of early Islam, and through more grief than most hearts are ever asked to hold. Few people stood so close to so much, or carried so much loss so quietly.

This is the story of Abdullah ibn Ja'far (may Allah be pleased with him): born to refugees in a foreign land, son of a beloved martyr, and a man the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ took into his own care when he was still a child.

sections.h2: A child born far from home
paras: Abdullah was not born in Makkah, and he was not born in Madinah. He was born in Abyssinia, the land the early Muslims called Habasha, across the sea.

His parents were Ja'far ibn Abi Talib and Asma bint Umays. They were among the believers who had fled there to escape the persecution of Makkah, crossing the water to find safety under the Negus, the just king who gave them shelter. Some of those families stayed for years. There were children born in that land, Abyssinian babies who would only later come to Madinah, and Abdullah was one of them.

His father, Ja'far, was the one who had spoken for the Muslims before the Negus, representing them with such eloquence and wisdom that he secured their place in that kingdom. The Prophet ﷺ loved him dearly. So Abdullah was born into a household at the very heart of the believing community, yet in a country none of them had chosen.

His people were Makkans, but he had never seen Makkah. His earliest world was the land that had taken his family in. It is a story that repeats in every age: a child born somewhere his parents never planned to be, because the place they loved had turned against them. That was the world Abdullah opened his eyes to.

sections.h2: The boat, and a father embraced
paras: One day the family boarded a boat. Not back to Makkah, the city Abdullah had never seen, but onward to Madinah, where the believers had now built a home.

He crossed those hard waters as a young child with his parents, Ja'far and Asma. The boats from Abyssinia arrived in Madinah at the time of the victory at Khaybar. When they reached the city, Abdullah watched the Prophet ﷺ run to meet his father and embrace him. The Prophet ﷺ said he did not know which made him happier: being reunited with his cousin Ja'far, or the victory that had just been won.
Imagine seeing that as a child. The most important man in your parents' world, and in the lives of all the believers around you, runs to your father with that kind of joy.

So Abdullah did not arrive as a stranger among followers. He arrived as family. He was not simply one who followed the Prophet ﷺ; he was kin to him, and the Prophet ﷺ noticed him at once and drew him close. That is how his life in Madinah began: held, and loved, from the first moment.

Then his father was sent far away, to the battle of Mutah, in the land of Jordan. There Ja'far became one of the most famous martyrs of that day. He held the banner until both his arms were cut from him on the field.

Think about how quickly the world was changing for this one boy. His life had already touched the persecution of Makkah, the migration to Abyssinia, and the unique journey to Madinah. Now his father had been martyred in the most painful way, thousands of miles from the home Abdullah had been born in, and thousands of miles from the city his family came from.

The Prophet ﷺ came to the grieving house. Abdullah's mother was weeping. With tears in his own eyes, the Prophet ﷺ held the boy and comforted him, and promised that he would care for him and that Allah would keep him under His divine protection.

Soon people began to greet Abdullah with a new name. They would say: welcome to the son of the man of two wings. For the Prophet ﷺ had told them that he saw Ja'far flying in Paradise. Allah had given him, in place of the two arms taken from him, two wings by which to fly through the Garden. A grieving boy now carried a name made entirely of his father's reward.

sections.h2: A family marked by loss, and by love
paras: After Ja'far, Abdullah's mother Asma married Abu Bakr (may Allah be pleased with him). On the way to the Farewell Hajj with the Prophet ﷺ, at the place of Miqat, she gave birth to Abdullah's half-brother, Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr. So his life now touched the Farewell Hajj of the Prophet ﷺ as well, the journey on which the believers made their pilgrimage at his side.

When Abu Bakr passed away, Asma washed his body, and then she married Ali (may Allah be pleased with him), who was Abdullah's own uncle. In time Abdullah himself would marry two of Ali's daughters, Zaynab and Umm Kulthum, at separate times. Ali, it is said, loved that the daughters of his house would marry into the family of Ja'far.

There is a lot of family to hold here, so it is worth holding it gently. Abdullah was the son of Ja'far and Asma. Ja'far passed, and Asma married Abu Bakr. Abu Bakr passed, and Asma married Ali. Each name carries great weight in this history, and each one was woven quietly into the story of a single man who rarely stood at the centre of any of it.
Abdullah was about ten years old when the Prophet ﷺ passed away. He was not too young to remember any of it. He was aware, and he carried it all clearly.

He remembered the crossing from Abyssinia to Madinah. He remembered the martyrdom of his father at Mutah. And he remembered the day the Prophet ﷺ left this world, the man who had stepped so gently into his life when his own father was gone.

You can imagine how hard that last loss must have been. The Prophet ﷺ had stepped into Abdullah's life after his father was gone, had held him in his grief and promised to care for him, and now he too had left this world. Few people of Abdullah's age had seen so much, or felt the absence of so many. The losses were not distant history to him. They were his own memories, kept for a lifetime.

sections.h2: Karbala, and a heart that kept breaking
paras: Abdullah's wife was Zaynab al-Kubra, the daughter of Ali and the sister of Husayn (may Allah be pleased with them). So when the terrible day of Karbala came, his life was bound to it. Husayn was martyred. Some of Abdullah's own sons were lost in that day. And his wife Zaynab was taken into captivity, made to walk through that entire painful aftermath.

It is hard to imagine carrying such a thing: to be in one place while, far away, your sons are killed and your wife is taken. Later, after Zaynab had passed, Abdullah married Umm Kulthum bint Ali, and her death too was a tragedy he had to witness.

Grief returned to him again and again, in form after form. Yet through every loss, he remained one of the most respected and beloved of his people. He is remembered, in the end, not for what was taken from him, but for the way he carried it.

sections.h2: An ending, and a thread that holds it together
paras: In the year 80 after the Hijra, there was a storm the people called al-Jahaf, the flood that swept everything away. It was not a wide disaster. It struck one small valley, near Makkah, during the days of Hajj, as the pilgrims were preparing for the standing at Arafah.

It came with such force that many of those pilgrims, and their belongings, were lost in it. One of them was Abdullah ibn Ja'far. So consider the shape of his life. Born to refugees in Abyssinia. Carried by boat to Madinah. His father martyred in a faraway land. The Prophet ﷺ passing in his childhood. Karbala touching his own household. And at the very end, he died as a pilgrim, on the road to worship his Lord.

It can be hard to know what to do with a life like this, so much grief gathered around one family, generation after generation. The household of the Prophet ﷺ carried sorrow upon sorrow, and Abdullah's line carried more than most. But there is a thread running through it that is worth holding onto. Sometimes Allah chooses certain families for difficulty. That was surely the case here. Yet they were not chosen for difficulty alone. They were chosen for it because they were promised reward.

The father whose arms were taken was given wings. The boy who buried so much was kept honoured and beloved until the day he died on the path of pilgrimage. The hardship and the reward came together, and one was the measure of the other. That is why a story so full of loss does not leave us in despair. It leaves us with something steadier: the trust that nothing a believer endures is wasted, and that Allah remembers what He asks of His servants.

lessons_intro: His story is not built on one great deed. It is built on a lifetime of loss met with patience, and a heart that stayed soft through all of it.

lessons.text: Abdullah lost his father, his sons, and those he loved, again and again. He is remembered not for being spared, but for how faithfully he carried what he was given.

lessons.bold: A kind word can hold a grieving heart.
lessons.text: The Prophet ﷺ came to a weeping house and held a fatherless boy. We can each be that presence for someone in their hardest hour.
A child born far from home was made family the moment he arrived. The people who feel like outsiders around us are waiting for someone to draw them close.

lessons.bold: Some are chosen for difficulty, and for reward.
lessons.text: The family of the Prophet ﷺ was tested deeply, yet the hardship carried a promise. Trial in a believing life is never wasted.

faq.a: He was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, the son of Ja'far ibn Abi Talib and Asma bint Umays. Born in Abyssinia to migrant believers, he was brought to Madinah as a child and raised within the Prophet's own family.

faq.q: Why is his father called the man of two wings?
faq.a: Ja'far was martyred at the battle of Mutah, where both his arms were cut off. The Prophet ﷺ said he saw Ja'far flying in Paradise, with two wings given to him in place of his arms. Abdullah was greeted as the son of the man of two wings.

faq.q: How did Abdullah ibn Ja'far's life connect to Karbala?
faq.a: His wife was Zaynab, the daughter of Ali and sister of Husayn. When Husayn was martyred at Karbala, some of Abdullah's own sons were lost, and his wife Zaynab was taken captive. The tragedy reached directly into his household.

faq.q: How did Abdullah ibn Ja'far die?
faq.a: In the year 80 after the Hijra, a violent storm known as al-Jahaf struck a small valley near Makkah during the days of Hajj. Many pilgrims were lost in the flood, and Abdullah was among them. He died as a pilgrim on the way to worship.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Abdullah did not choose where he was born, or which losses would find him. Almost nothing about his life was in his hands. What was his to choose was how he met it: with patience that did not harden into bitterness, and a heart that stayed tender through grief after grief.

Most of us are not asked to face what he faced. But we are all asked the same quiet question, in smaller ways, on ordinary days: when something is taken, will we let it close us, or will we carry it with grace and still reach for others? Ask Allah for that kind of heart today, and let Abdullah's life show you what it looks like.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Abdullah ibn Ja'far (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
He was not born in Makkah.

He was not born in Madinah.

He was born in Abyssinia, across the sea, the child of refugees who had fled persecution.

As a boy he crossed the water by boat to Madinah.

He watched the Prophet ﷺ run to embrace his father, Ja'far, with tears of joy.

Then his father was sent to Mutah, far away in Jordan.

There Ja'far was martyred. Both of his arms were cut from him on the field.

The Prophet ﷺ came to the grieving house.

With tears in his own eyes, he held the fatherless boy and promised to care for him.

Soon people greeted him with a new name:

"Welcome, son of the man of two wings."

The Prophet ﷺ had seen Ja'far flying in Paradise, given wings in place of his arms.

His life touched almost every great event of early Islam.

The migration. The Farewell Hajj. The loss of the Prophet ﷺ when he was about ten.

Karbala reached his own home. His wife Zaynab was taken captive. Some of his sons were lost.

In the year 80 after Hijra, he died as a pilgrim, in a flood on the days of Hajj.

**Grief does not cancel honour.**

He is remembered not for being spared, but for how faithfully he carried what he was given.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Abdullah ibn Ja'far (may Allah be pleased with him) was born in Abyssinia, the child of believers who had fled the persecution of Makkah. He had never seen the city his family came from.

As a boy he crossed the sea to Madinah, where the Prophet ﷺ took him into his own care. His father, Ja'far, was martyred at Mutah and called "the man of two wings." His life would go on to touch the Farewell Hajj, the passing of the Prophet ﷺ, and the tragedy of Karbala, which reached his own household. He died, at the end, as a pilgrim on the road to Hajj.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The boy the Prophet ﷺ held in a grieving house
email.alt_subject: Born a refugee, raised as family
email.preview: Abdullah ibn Ja'far was born far from home, and his life touched almost every great event of early Islam.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Some companions are remembered for one famous moment. Abdullah ibn Ja'far (may Allah be pleased with him) is remembered for a whole life that touched almost everything.

He was not born in Makkah, and not in Madinah. He was born in Abyssinia, across the sea, the child of believers who had fled the persecution of Makkah. His first home was a place of refuge. He had never even seen the city his family came from.

As a young boy, he crossed the water by boat to Madinah. There he watched the Prophet ﷺ run to embrace his father, Ja'far, so glad to be reunited that he said he did not know which made him happier: his cousin's return, or the victory at Khaybar.

Then his father was sent to the battle of Mutah, far away in Jordan, and was martyred there. Both of his arms were cut from him on the field. The Prophet ﷺ came to the grieving house, and with tears in his own eyes he held the fatherless boy and promised to care for him.

Soon people began to greet Abdullah with a new name: the son of the man of two wings. For the Prophet ﷺ had said he saw Ja'far flying in Paradise, given two wings in place of the arms he had lost.

Grief returned to Abdullah more than once. His wife was Zaynab, the sister of Husayn, and the tragedy of Karbala reached his own household. At the end of his life he died as a pilgrim, lost in a flood during the days of Hajj.

Through all of it, he was remembered not for being spared, but for how faithfully he carried what he was given. It is a reminder that sometimes Allah chooses certain people for difficulty, and that the difficulty is matched by a reward. The father who lost his arms was given wings.

There is a quiet lesson in that for any of us. Grief does not have to harden the heart. It can be carried with patience, and with kindness still left over for others.

May Allah be pleased with him, and soften our hearts the way his stayed soft.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Abdullah ibn Ja'far (may Allah be pleased with him) was not born in Makkah, and not in Madinah. He was born in Abyssinia, across the sea, the child of believers who had fled the persecution of their city. His first home was a place of refuge, and he had never once seen the land his family came from.

As a young boy he crossed the hard waters by boat, sailing to Madinah where the believers had built a home. When they arrived, he watched the Prophet ﷺ run to his father, Ja'far, and embrace him, so glad that he said he did not know which made him happier: his cousin's return, or the victory at Khaybar.
So Abdullah did not arrive as a stranger. He arrived as family, noticed and loved from the first moment.

Then his father was sent far away, to the battle of Mutah in the land of Jordan, and there Ja'far was martyred. Both of his arms were cut from him on the field. The Prophet ﷺ came to the grieving house, where Abdullah's mother was weeping, and with tears in his own eyes he held the fatherless boy and promised that he would care for him.

Soon the people greeted Abdullah with a new name. They would say: welcome to the son of the man of two wings. For the Prophet ﷺ had told them he saw Ja'far flying in Paradise, given two wings in place of the arms he had lost. A grieving child now carried a name made of his father's reward.

Abdullah was about ten when the Prophet ﷺ passed away, old enough to remember it all. And grief would return to him again. His wife was Zaynab, the sister of Husayn, and the tragedy of Karbala reached into his own home. Some of his sons were lost, and Zaynab was taken captive.

In the year 80 after the Hijra, a storm called al-Jahaf swept through a small valley near Makkah during the days of Hajj. Many pilgrims were lost in the flood, and Abdullah was one of them. He died as a pilgrim, on the road to worship his Lord.
storybook.pullquote: Welcome, son of the man of two wings.
storybook.reflection: Almost nothing about Abdullah's life was in his hands: not where he was born, not the losses that found him. What was his to choose was how he carried them. When something is taken from us, we face the same quiet question he did. We can let grief close our hearts, or we can carry it with patience and still reach for others.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
